event: Polynesian voyagers using double-hulled canoes settle the islands, becoming the ancestors of the Maori, the last major landmass to be reached by humans.
Maori develop distinct iwi (tribal) and hapu (subtribe) societies, hunting the giant flightless moa and building fortified pa settlements.
Overhunting and habitat loss drive the moa to extinction, prompting Maori to shift toward fishing, kumara cultivation, and bird snaring.
Dutch navigator Abel Tasman becomes the first European to sight New Zealand, but a deadly clash with Maori at Golden Bay deters him from landing.
British explorer James Cook circumnavigates and charts both main islands aboard the Endeavour, opening the country to European contact.
Anglican missionary Samuel Marsden establishes the first Christian mission and preaches the first sermon at Oihi in the Bay of Islands.
The introduction of muskets ignites the Musket Wars, decades of intertribal conflict that reshape Maori territory and cause heavy casualties.
Northern Maori chiefs sign He Whakaputanga, the Declaration of Independence of the United Tribes of New Zealand.
The Treaty of Waitangi is signed between the British Crown and Maori chiefs, founding the colony and remaining New Zealand's foundational document.
New Zealand becomes a separate British Crown colony, no longer administered as a dependency of New South Wales.
The Northern (Flagstaff) War breaks out as Hone Heke and Kawiti challenge British authority in the Bay of Islands.
The New Zealand Constitution Act grants representative self-government, creating a General Assembly and provincial councils.
The Taranaki War erupts over disputed land sales, beginning the wider New Zealand Wars between the Crown and Maori.
British forces invade the Waikato, and the New Zealand Settlements Act enables large-scale confiscation of Maori land.
The capital is moved from Auckland to Wellington for its more central location.
Four Maori parliamentary seats are established, giving Maori men the vote ahead of universal male suffrage.
The Education Act introduces free, compulsory, and secular primary schooling nationwide.
The first shipment of frozen meat sails to Britain, launching an export economy that would dominate New Zealand for a century.
New Zealand becomes the first self-governing country in the world to grant women the right to vote in national elections.
The Old-age Pensions Act establishes one of the world's first state pension schemes.
New Zealand is proclaimed a Dominion within the British Empire, gaining greater autonomy from the United Kingdom.
New Zealand and Australian troops land at Gallipoli, forging the ANZAC legend and a lasting sense of national identity.
The global influenza pandemic kills around 9,000 New Zealanders, including a disproportionate number of Maori.
A devastating earthquake destroys Napier and Hastings, killing 256 people in the country's deadliest natural disaster.
The first Labour government is elected and builds a comprehensive welfare state during the Great Depression.
New Zealand enters World War II alongside Britain, sending forces to North Africa, Italy, and the Pacific.
New Zealand becomes a founding member of the United Nations, signing the UN Charter.
New Zealand adopts the Statute of Westminster, formally achieving full legislative independence from Britain.
New Zealand signs the ANZUS security treaty with Australia and the United States.
New Zealander Edmund Hillary, with Tenzing Norgay, becomes one of the first to reach the summit of Mount Everest.
Western Samoa, formerly administered by New Zealand, becomes the first Pacific nation to gain independence.
Britain's entry into the European Economic Community forces New Zealand to diversify its export markets away from the UK.
The Treaty of Waitangi Act creates the Waitangi Tribunal to investigate Maori grievances against the Crown.
The Springbok rugby tour by apartheid-era South Africa sparks mass protests and deeply divides New Zealand society.
The fourth Labour government launches sweeping free-market economic reforms known as Rogernomics.
French agents bomb the Greenpeace ship Rainbow Warrior in Auckland Harbour, killing a photographer and straining relations with France.
The Nuclear Free Zone Act bans nuclear-armed and nuclear-powered ships, effectively suspending New Zealand's ANZUS obligations with the US, and makes Maori an official language.
The Crown signs a landmark settlement with Waikato-Tainui, including an apology and compensation for 19th-century land confiscations.
New Zealand holds its first general election under the proportional Mixed-Member Proportional (MMP) voting system.
Helen Clark becomes the country's first elected female prime minister, leading a Labour-led government for nine years.
The Maori Party is founded amid the foreshore and seabed controversy over coastal ownership rights.
A magnitude 7.1 earthquake strikes the Canterbury region near Christchurch, beginning a destructive seismic sequence.
A magnitude 6.3 aftershock devastates central Christchurch, killing 185 people and toppling the cathedral.
New Zealand legalises same-sex marriage, becoming the first country in the Asia-Pacific region to do so.
Te Awa Tupua legislation grants the Whanganui River legal personhood, a world-first recognition of a river's rights.
A terrorist attack on two Christchurch mosques kills 51 worshippers, prompting swift gun-law reform under Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern.
New Zealand's strict elimination strategy and border closures keep COVID-19 deaths among the lowest in the developed world during the pandemic's first year.
Cyclone Gabrielle inflicts catastrophic flooding and damage across the North Island, underscoring the country's exposure to climate-driven extreme weather.
